commits.body:
Final tightening from the wrap pass. No behaviour changes — all three
edits are documentation and comments.

1. supabase/migrations/20260421000001 header: reworded the
   "Application path" comment so it doesn't read like a one-off event.
   The original wording ("applied via management API on 2026-04-21 as
   postgres") made sense for this session but looked historical for
   anyone running `supabase db push` on a fresh DB later. New wording
   explains the idempotency invariant (both `CREATE OR REPLACE
   FUNCTION` and `CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER` need only the `TRIGGER`
   privilege, which `postgres` has on this table) and keeps the
   first-apply date as a footnote.

2. docs/USER_ACTIONS.md: two additions to the support-ticket section:
   - Structured 7-step post-resolution cleanup that names every
     artifact to delete/update (migration file, ledger row, test
     assertions, CLAUDE.md project-status line + Known production
     risks section, SESSION_NOTES, TODO entry for scheduled CI).
     Previously said "also delete migration 20260421000001 from repo"
     in one run-on sentence; easy to miss half the steps.
   - **Emergency brake snippet**: if the bridge trigger ever fires
     for a legitimate write (e.g. a future Supabase maintenance op
     running as a role we didn't allowlist), we can't DROP TRIGGER
     (ownership wall) but we own the function, so
     CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION to a `RETURN NULL` body neutralizes
     the trigger in one statement. Clearly labelled as re-opening
     the DoS window — only for active investigation. Restore path
     is re-running the migration file.

3. docs/TODO.md: new entry for migrating
   src/components/pwa/__tests__/IconCacheDisclosure.test.tsx off
   `react-test-renderer`. The library is officially deprecated on
   React 19 and will be removed in a future major. Current test
   works via act() wrapping + IS_REACT_ACT_ENVIRONMENT=true (fixed in
   d8bf841) but that's a bridge. Proper fix:
   @testing-library/react-native. Noted that
   DebugRootBoundary.test.tsx already avoids the library by
   instantiating class components directly, so no migration needed
   there.

- .env is in .gitignore (both `.env` and `.env*.local` lines)

  (`supabase db reset`), not session-specific — no update needed

Two additions from the post-lockdown wrap pass:

1. scripts/__tests__/secret-leakage.test.ts — walks src/, scripts/,
   public/, app/ and fails if any file contains a JWT-shaped token
   (`eyJ[...]{30,}.eyJ[...]{10,}`). Protects against the category of
   bug where a service-role key accidentally gets committed to
   client-side code — far worse than the spatial_ref_sys exposure
   since service_role bypasses RLS on every table.

   Verified the detector by temporarily dropping a fake JWT into
   src/_test-leak-temp.ts, running the test (fails with file+line),
   removing it, re-running (passes). Standing state: 0 findings —
   repo has no committed JWTs anywhere.

   Current baseline (as of this commit): anon key lives only in .env
   / .env.example (as it should — ANON key is meant to be public and
   bundled at build time); no service_role key anywhere in the repo;
   the only "service_role" string is an allowlist mention in the
   migration 20260421000001 header.

2. docs/TODO.md — new entry: schedule periodic runs of
   spatial-ref-sys-lockdown.test.ts in CI. The trigger-based DoS
   mitigation installed in 19ed26d could theoretically be dropped by
   a platform operation (PostGIS upgrade, table rebuild) without
   any signal. A daily cron run of this one test would catch a
   regression within ~24h. Needs SUPABASE_ACCESS_TOKEN_CLAUDE_WEB
   as a CI secret. Becomes unnecessary once Supabase support lands
   the RLS fix (robustly preserved, no monitoring needed).

  history append-only via INSERT-only policy. No regressions.

  spatial_ref_sys (re-confirmed).

commits.subject: test(migrations): add return-type-drift detector to migration-files test
commits.body:
Complements the filename-uniqueness check with a second static
assertion: every CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION across all migration files
must either keep its RETURNS signature identical to any prior
definition, or be preceded (same file or any earlier file, before the
new CREATE) by a matching DROP FUNCTION. Postgres refuses to change a
function's return type via CREATE OR REPLACE alone — we shipped this
exact bug in 20260322000002 (widened bulk_insert_stations from 2 cols
to 3) and only caught it when this session tried to push.

Detector walks events (CREATE and DROP) per function name in
chronological file order. On CREATE: if returnType differs from the
last-seen for the same name AND no DROP has been seen since the last
CREATE, record a drift. On DROP: reset the per-function tracker so
the next CREATE is treated as fresh.

Parsing is regex-based against our plpgsql migration style (strip
line comments first, then match CREATE...RETURNS...LANGUAGE/AS and
DROP FUNCTION [IF EXISTS]). Avoids pulling in a full Postgres
parser like libpg_query.

Verified the detector actually catches regressions by temporarily
removing the DROP FUNCTION IF EXISTS line from migration
20260322000002, running the test, and confirming it fails with a
diff-output naming the previous/new files and return types. Then
restored.

4 tests in this file now (was 3): filename count, timestamp format,
unique versions, return-type consistency.

Tags: testing, migrations, static-analysis

Impact: internal
Risk: low

commits.subject: fix(db): trigger-based write lockdown on spatial_ref_sys + live tests
commits.body:
Closes the DoS window on public.spatial_ref_sys without needing the
Supabase support ticket. The canonical fix (ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE
RLS via migration 20260409000001) still needs supabase_admin and
remains in the repo, but this trigger is sufficient defense-in-depth.

How it sidesteps the ownership wall that blocks RLS:
Different DDL statements check different privileges. ALTER TABLE
requires ownership (table owned by supabase_admin), but CREATE TRIGGER
needs only the TRIGGER privilege — which `postgres` holds here
(verified via has_table_privilege). CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER in PG
14+ also avoids the DROP TRIGGER requirement (which would have needed
table ownership). The trigger itself adds no structural change to
spatial_ref_sys, just an external gate on writes.

Trigger body raises insufficient_privilege (42501) for any caller not
in ('supabase_admin', 'postgres'). RAISE EXCEPTION in a BEFORE trigger
aborts the statement before any row is touched.

Verified live against the remote DB:
- anon INSERT: blocked (42501 with our custom message)
- anon UPDATE on SRID 4326: blocked
- anon DELETE: blocked
- anon TRUNCATE: blocked
- authenticated INSERT: blocked
- anon SELECT on SRID 4326: still returns the EPSG row
- postgres round-trip insert/delete: still works
- find_stations_in_radius(-26.2, 28.05, 50): returns 39 stations

Applied to remote 2026-04-21 via management API as postgres.

The test file uses the management API with SET ROLE anon/authenticated
rather than PostgREST over fetch() because the dev env's outbound HTTP
stack is flaky (intermittent proxy 503s); the management API is
consistent. Tests skip gracefully when SUPABASE_ACCESS_TOKEN_CLAUDE_WEB
isn't available (local dev without credentials, third-party contributors).

commits.subject: revert(db): reinstate spatial_ref_sys RLS migration — it's a real DoS risk
commits.body:
Earlier in this session I closed this migration as "accepted-not-fixed"
on the reasoning that spatial_ref_sys is PostGIS reference data and
our app never writes to it. The user's follow-up — "is it ok to leave
it?" — prompted me to actually test the attack surface instead of
reasoning about it. The reasoning was wrong.

Verified live (test rows inserted and cleaned up):
- anon has table-level INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, TRUNCATE grants
- A POST to /rest/v1/spatial_ref_sys with the public anon key returns
  HTTP 201 with the row inserted
- REVOKE by postgres silently no-ops (supabase_admin was the grantor;
  postgres lacks grant authority — same ownership wall as RLS)

Impact: any attacker with the anon key (bundled in the web app JS
bundle — publicly visible) can TRUNCATE the table or corrupt SRID
4326, breaking find_stations_in_radius and every ST_Distance /
ST_Transform call in the app. Full DoS with one curl, no auth.

The migration file's SELECT-only RLS policy implicitly denies writes
(under RLS, operations without a matching policy default-deny, so the
existing table GRANTs become moot). It's the right fix; we just can't
apply it from any surface that runs as `postgres`.

Changes:
- Restored supabase/migrations/20260409000001_enable_rls_spatial_ref_sys.sql
  with a rewritten header explaining the full platform constraint and
  why the SELECT-only policy is sufficient.
- CLAUDE.md: replaced the "Accepted lint warnings" section with
  "Known production risks" so this can't age into accepted-by-default.
  Project-status line now flags it as an open risk.
- docs/USER_ACTIONS.md: added a support-ticket template with the exact
  SQL for the support engineer to run as supabase_admin.
- docs/AI_MISTAKES.md: new entry — don't grade security issues by
  reasoning when one curl would prove it in seconds.
- docs/SESSION_NOTES.md: reflects the corrected outcome.

commits.subject: fix(db): accept spatial_ref_sys RLS lint warning; remove unapplyable migration
commits.body:
Tested live: Studio SQL Editor fails with the same 42501 as the
management API and `supabase db push`. I was wrong earlier in this
session when I claimed Studio was the escalation path — on this
project it runs as `postgres`, same as the others, and `postgres` is
not a member of `supabase_admin` so `SET ROLE` doesn't help.

Checked the full escalation surface before giving up:
- No shipped SECURITY DEFINER helper owned by supabase_admin covers
  RLS/ALTER (searched pg_proc for %rls%, %spatial%, %alter%, %admin%,
  %privileg% — zero matches).
- supabase_privileged_role isn't a member of supabase_admin and
  doesn't have UPDATE on spatial_ref_sys.

The only real path to flip the bit is a Supabase support ticket.
Not worth it — spatial_ref_sys is PostGIS public reference data, our
app never writes to it, and "writes denied by RLS" is already
enforced by table ownership (we can't write to a table we don't own).
The security value of enabling RLS here is nil.

Changes:
- Deleted supabase/migrations/20260409000001_enable_rls_spatial_ref_sys.sql
  so it doesn't sit permanently unapplied and mislead new sessions.
- CLAUDE.md: project-status line updated (17/17 applied + 1 accepted);
  AI Notes entry corrected to reflect that Studio does NOT escalate
  either; new "Accepted lint warnings" section documents the decision.
- docs/USER_ACTIONS.md: removed the now-obsolete "Enable RLS on
  spatial_ref_sys" section.
- docs/SESSION_NOTES.md + docs/AI_MISTAKES.md: updated to record the
  correct outcome and the mistaken Studio-privileges assumption.

commits.body:
The prior version errored on re-run with "policy already exists" and
"duplicate key value" on the schema_migrations INSERT. If a paste into
Studio partially succeeded (ALTER ok, CREATE POLICY failed for any
reason), the user couldn't safely re-run the same block.

Changes to the migration file:
- DROP POLICY IF EXISTS before CREATE POLICY so re-runs don't error on
  the policy name. ENABLE RLS is already a no-op when already enabled.
- Header note explaining the ownership constraint (supabase_admin vs
  postgres) and the Studio SQL Editor escape hatch.

  ledger insert) so the user runs one paste, not two — prior version
  sent them to open the migration file + a separate INSERT snippet.
- Stronger verification query (checks RLS flag, policy count, and
  ledger row in one call).
- Documented the platform-change fallback (if Studio also errors 42501,
  open a support ticket — don't retry via CLI).

commits.subject: fix(tests): repair 8 pre-existing failures surfaced after npm install
commits.body:
None of the 23 tests added in 3a60a28 had ever been executed — its
session notes said tests were "not yet executed" because npm install
wasn't available in that env. Running `npm install && npm test` here
surfaced 8 failures across two suites, all shipping to main.

IconCacheDisclosure (6 failures): react-test-renderer is deprecated
on React 19. `TestRenderer.create()` returns a renderer that's
unmounted before the test can read `.root`, and the environment
wasn't flagged as act-compatible. Fix: set
IS_REACT_ACT_ENVIRONMENT=true and wrap every create() in act() via
a renderComponent() helper. react-test-renderer is still functional
inside act(), so no dep change needed. Added @types/react-test-renderer
(was silently missing — `typeof import('react-test-renderer')` was
resolving to any, suppressing TS7006 checks on findAll callbacks).

inject-icon-hashes (2 failures):

1. Idempotent re-run emitted a WARNING treating "0 replacements" as
   format drift, even when HTML was already versioned. Script now
   tracks whether HTML contains already-versioned refs for known
   icons and only warns when neither bare nor versioned matches —
   the actual drift case. The existing "format drift" test still
   passes because its fixture has neither.
2. "Missing precached icon" test tried to `rmSync` favicon.png, but
   favicon was dropped from the pipeline in 19821c4 and isn't in the
   fixture. Switched to icon-192.png (still in SW_EXPECTED_URLS).

Unrelated to the migration-sync work on this branch — but surfaced
once `npm install` exposed the test runner, and fixing them here
rather than opening another branch avoids leaving main on a
chronically-red suite.

commits.subject: fix(pwa): drop favicon from icon-hash pipeline (Expo emit path unpredictable)
commits.body:
Vercel build failed on commit 3f2a479:

  [inject-icon-hashes] precached icon(s) missing from dist/:
    '/favicon.png'
  SW install would fail on cache.addAll()
  Error: Command '...' exited with 1

Root cause: app.json web.favicon points at assets/images/favicon.png,
but Expo's web export does NOT copy it verbatim — the emitted path is
version-dependent (.ico conversion vs PNG passthrough vs auto-discovery
only). The tiered fail-loud I added in the edges pass then (correctly)
refused to ship a PRECACHE list pointing at a non-existent file.

Resolution: favicon is outside the icon-hash pipeline. Removed from
ICON_URLS, SW_EXPECTED_URLS, PRECACHE_ASSETS, and the test fixture.
+html.tsx now references /favicon.ico (best guess at what Expo emits
+ matches browser auto-discovery); vercel.json passes both .ico and
.png through so whichever Expo emits isn't swallowed by the SPA
catch-all. Favicon is small + HTTP-cached aggressively + dominated by
the OS icon cache for installed PWAs, so skipping ?v= on it is an
acceptable trade.

Verified end-to-end against a fixture with zero favicon files in
dist/ — build chain (expo export -> inject-icon-hashes ->
inject-sw-version) exits 0, all other icons versioned correctly.

commits.subject: perf(pwa): content-hash SW_BUILD over dist/_expo/static/ bundles
commits.body:
SW_BUILD used to be an ISO timestamp injected on every deploy, which
meant STATIC_CACHE / DYNAMIC_CACHE names rotated on every build — even
when no shipped content changed. The activate handler's name-based
cleanup then wiped every user's cache on every deploy, defeating the
purpose of precaching, and every user got an "update available" prompt
for noop deploys.

New approach: SHA-256 over every file under dist/_expo/static/ (sorted
recursive walk, filename + bytes in the digest), truncated to 12 hex
chars. Same content → same version → identical SW bytes → browser's
byte-compare detects no change → no spurious update prompt, cache
persists. Changed content → new version → SW updates as expected.

Fail-loud if the __SW_BUILD_VERSION__ placeholder is missing (idempotent
re-run on same dist/ or public/sw.js drift).

commits.subject: fix(pwa): wire icon-hash injector into Vercel build + mark favicon immutable
commits.body:
Vercel uses buildCommand directly, not `npm run build`, so production
deploys were skipping inject-icon-hashes.mjs and shipping un-versioned
icon URLs. Every cache layer below the OS would keep serving the old
icon indefinitely — the exact failure mode the pattern doc warns about.

Also mark /favicon.png as immutable since the URL is now content-addressed
via ?v=<hash>. /assets/images/* already gets the treatment via the
/assets/(.*) rule; /favicon.png was falling through to max-age=0 in the
catch-all. Bytes change → URL changes → browsers/CDN can safely cache
the versioned form for a year.

commits.subject: feat(pwa): content-hash icon URLs to bust browser/CDN/SW caches
commits.body:
When icons change, un-versioned URLs survive "clear site data" and
PWA reinstall because every cache layer (HTTP, CDN, SW precache,
WebAPK) keys off URL identity. Add a post-export script that hashes
each referenced icon and rewrites HTML link tags, manifest.json
icons[], and sw.js PRECACHE_ASSETS to ?v=<8-char-sha256>.

Implementation choices:
- Post-export rewrite (not +html.tsx at render) — matches the existing
  inject-sw-version.mjs pattern; keeps Metro bundling of +html.tsx
  unchanged.
- Precache the versioned URL directly (not strip ?v= on lookup) so
  the custom sw.js's caches.match() stays exact-URL.
- Fix CACHE_FIRST_PATTERNS regex to accept query strings — without
  this, versioned icons fall through to network-first and break
  offline.
- Collapsed disclosure in InstallInstructionsModal surfaces the one
  cache layer the web can't fix (OS home-screen/launcher icon).

Verified via dry-run against a simulated dist/: 3 HTML rewrites, 3
manifest icons, 3 sw.js precache entries, idempotent on re-run.

commits.subject: fix(debug): LogRow renders event + details through redactUrls (security/SEC1)
commits.body:
The Env tab redacts env.url at construction and debugGenerateReport
runs a final redactUrls sweep before the clipboard copy — so Env-tab
screenshots and pasted reports are both screenshot-safe. But the
Log tab's on-screen rendering of entry.event and entry.details did
NOT redact. A screenshot of the open pill's Log tab could leak URLs
that the report-copy path would redact.

Concrete leak:
1. App does console.error('Failed:', new Error('POST https://api/?apikey=X ...'))
   or debugAdd('supabase', 'error', 'Request failed',
     { url: 'https://api.com/?apikey=SECRET' })
2. Entry lands in buffer; event + details contain the URL.
3. User opens DebugPill → Log tab. URL + apikey visible on screen.
4. Screenshot → leaked.

Fix: apply redactUrls in LogRow at render time to both
  - entry.event (string)
  - the stringified entry.details (whether details is already a
    string or an object that needs safeStringify)

Matches the screenshot-safety pattern already applied to env.url.
The raw entry.details are still unmodified in the in-memory buffer
(preserves programmatic access for any future debug tooling); only
the displayed strings go through redaction.

New test covers the exact chain LogRow uses:
  redactUrls(typeof details === 'string' ? details : safeStringify(details))
Three assertions: structured detail with URL, string detail with URL,
event string with URL. All three paths must not leak the query.

Other security findings from the sweep:
- window.__debugPushError / __debugClearLoadTimer exposed globals:
  low-risk, XSS-safe by design (textContent, not innerHTML).
- 'unsafe-inline' CSP for scripts: pre-existing tradeoff, not touched
  by this branch.
- Supabase anon key + Mapbox token: intentionally public per each
  platform's security model (RLS server-side; URL-restricted token).
- localStorage writes: timestamps + browser name, no PII.
- Linking.openURL / window.open: hardcoded URLs only, no user-input
  redirect surface.
- eval / new Function: none.
- Exposed secrets in branch code: none.

commits.subject: fix(debug): redactUrls preserves trailing sentence punctuation (edges/EDG1)
commits.body:
The existing char classes in both redactUrls passes only excluded
whitespace and URL-delimiter chars (`#'"<>`). That greedily consumed
trailing sentence punctuation — the `.` at end of a prose sentence,
commas before conjunctions, closing parens / brackets wrapping the
URL, exclamation marks. Before:

  "Reported at https://a.com/?t=1."    → "...https://a.com/?[redacted]"
  "Check https://a.com/?t=1, thanks"   → "...https://a.com/?[redacted] thanks"
  "See (https://a.com/?t=1)"           → "See (https://a.com/?[redacted]"
  "[https://a.com/?t=1]"               → "[https://a.com/?[redacted]"
  "Error: https://a.com/?t=1!"         → "...https://a.com/?[redacted]"

The debug report is pasted into support chats; characters from the
user's prose going missing makes it look like a bug on the report
side.

Fix: extend the regex char class with a conditional — `.,;!)]` are
excluded ONLY WHEN followed by whitespace or end-of-string. Inside
URL values they still match (e.g., `?v=1.2.3` keeps the dots inside
the redaction; OAuth JWT fragments `#access_token=abc.def.ghi` keep
all three dots in the redacted body). After:

  "Reported at https://a.com/?t=1."    → "...https://a.com/?[redacted]."
  "Check https://a.com/?t=1, thanks"   → "...https://a.com/?[redacted], thanks"
  "See (https://a.com/?t=1)"           → "See (https://a.com/?[redacted])"
  "[https://a.com/?t=1]"               → "[https://a.com/?[redacted]]"
  "Error: https://a.com/?t=1!"         → "...https://a.com/?[redacted]!"
  "https://a.com/?v=1.2.3"             → "https://a.com/?[redacted]" (unchanged)
  "https://a.com/?v=1.2.3."            → "https://a.com/?[redacted]." (period preserved)
  "https://app.com/cb#access_token=abc.def.ghi"
                                       → "https://app.com/cb#[redacted]" (unchanged)
  "See https://app.com/#access_token=abc."
                                       → "See https://app.com/#[redacted]."

Regex pattern used for each URL body segment:

commits.subject: fix(debug): cancel PwaDiagnosticsTab diagnostics on unmount (race/R1)
commits.body:
The diagnostics runner (runPwaDiagnostics) is async — fetches
/manifest.json and awaits navigator.serviceWorker.getRegistration().
Each step calls the `add` callback (which is setLines) to append a
result line. If the user switches debug tabs, collapses the pill,
or unmounts mid-flight, subsequent add() calls setState on an
unmounted component. React 18+ silently drops the update, so there
was no visible warning — but the closure + pending I/O still held
memory until the promises resolved.

Fix: `let cancelled = false;` flag set in the effect's cleanup
function. The add callback short-circuits when cancelled, so the
React state update path terminates immediately on unmount even
though the underlying fetch/getRegistration promises continue to
settle in the background.

Kept the rest of the audit findings as confirmed-correct:
- setInterval in usePWAUpdate intentionally page-lifetime.
- Boot pill clear() vs window.error race: safe either way (JS single-
  threaded; both orderings leave a consistent state).
- DebugPill subscriber closure: [] deps + module-stable references,
  no stale-closure risk.
- consoleCapture reentrant flag: local scope, shared between
  wrappedError and wrappedWarn, try/finally managed.
- debugRootMount DOMContentLoaded + `{ once: true }` + idempotency
  flag: triple-safe.

commits.subject: fix(debug): 4 bugs from ship/bugs trigger
commits.body:
B1 — redactUrls step 2 was over-redacting hash-router URLs with
query-style fragment params. `https://app.com/#/settings?tab=profile`
was getting redacted to `#[redacted]` because the fragment contained
`=`. Hash-router convention always starts with `/` after the `#`;
OAuth implicit-flow callbacks never do. Added a `(?!\/)` negative
lookahead to step 2 so hash-routes are preserved while
`#access_token=…` is still redacted. Three new assertions in the
redactUrls test block cover the new behaviour + prove the OAuth
path still works.

B2 — RECOVERY_TIMEOUT_MS (15000) was a literal inside the
recoveryScript template, while BOOT_PILL_TIMEOUT_MS was a named
constant. The comment on BOOT_PILL_TIMEOUT_MS documented a
must-stay-greater-than invariant with RECOVERY_TIMEOUT_MS — but
RECOVERY_TIMEOUT_MS didn't exist as a symbol so the invariant could
silently break. Extracted RECOVERY_TIMEOUT_MS alongside
BOOT_PILL_TIMEOUT_MS, interpolated into the recoveryScript template,
and made the relationship explicit in both comments.

B7 — DebugRootBoundary.componentDidCatch was discarding React's
errorInfo.componentStack. React's componentStack is more useful than
the raw JS stack for locating the crashing component (JS stack shows
internal React frames; componentStack shows the React tree path).
Updated the signature to accept errorInfo and include componentStack
in the structured details. Test updated to pass errorInfo and assert
componentStack lands in details; also added null-componentStack
coverage for React cases where the stack isn't available.

B10 — installConsoleCapture() ran during Expo's static rendering
(Node process, Platform.OS === 'web'), which patched Node's console
for the duration of the SSR process. Harmless but wasteful (leaked
debug entries nobody reads). Added a `typeof document === 'undefined'`
gate to skip SSR. Both jest test files now stub a minimal `document`
object before importing debugLog so the tests still exercise the
web-only install path; commented on why.

commits.subject: fix(debug): notifyAll re-entry guard + cascade bound
commits.body:
Previously, a subscriber that synchronously called debugAdd inside its
own callback would re-enter notifyAll mid-iteration. Best case: each
re-entry iterates all subscribers again, stack depth grows with each
nested call. Worst case: a subscriber that always calls debugAdd
infinite-recurses to stack overflow. None of the current subscribers
do this, but the latent concern has been noted in-session for several
refactors.

Fix:
- `notifying` flag tracks whether notifyAll is mid-iteration. If
  re-entered, sets pendingNotify and returns — the entry is still
  added (debugAdd adds to the buffer before calling notifyAll), and
  the outer do-while picks up pendingNotify and runs another pass so
  every subscriber still sees the new snapshot. Single-pass iteration
  semantics preserved.
- NOTIFY_CASCADE_MAX = 10 bounds pathological subscribers that ALWAYS
  call debugAdd (which would otherwise spin forever in the do-while).
  10 rounds is way beyond any realistic cascade — Fast-Refresh errors,
  PropType warnings, etc. rarely stack more than 2–3 deep.

New tests (2):
- A subscriber that cascades 3 times: all 3 'cascade-N' entries land
  in the buffer, no RangeError, no hang.
- A subscriber that infinitely re-triggers: entry count is finite
  (bounded by the cascade guard) and control returns normally.

commits.subject: fix(debug): sentinel-guard global error listeners against Fast Refresh duplication
commits.body:
The console-patch block has had a __debugPatched sentinel from day one
to avoid re-wrapping console.error/warn on repeat module execution.
The two window-level listeners right above it did not. On Fast Refresh
(dev) or any other repeat module evaluation, each re-execute would
addEventListener again — silently multiplying every captured error by
the number of re-executes.

Fix: mirror the console-patch pattern with a __debugGlobalListenersInstalled
sentinel on globalThis. Registration runs only when the flag is unset;
re-executions see it set and skip.

New tests (2):
- Asserts that on first module load, addEventListener was called once
  each for 'error' and 'unhandledrejection'.
- Under jest.isolateModules + re-require, asserts zero new
  addEventListener calls for those types.

Jest plumbing: Node's globalThis has no addEventListener (web-only API),
so the production `typeof globalThis.addEventListener !== 'undefined'`
guard would have made the branch a dead no-op under jest. Install a
jest.fn stub BEFORE the first require so the branch actually executes
and idempotency is observable.

commits.subject: feat(debug): redact OAuth-style fragment tokens + mention redaction in CLAUDE.md
commits.body:
Two small follow-ups on the URL-redaction pass:

1. Redact fragments that contain `=`. OAuth implicit-flow callbacks
   deliver access tokens via the fragment (`#access_token=…`); the
   previous pass preserved fragments wholesale, which meant the one
   well-known fragment-carried-secret shape was still leaking.

   Tight heuristic: `=` is the discriminator. Section anchors
   (`#chapter`) and hash-routes (`#/settings`) don't contain `=` and
   remain readable — they're useful for debugging and not secret.

2. CLAUDE.md's debug-system project-status bullet now mentions
   redaction, so a reader scanning project state knows about it
   without having to grep the code.

Implementation:
- Second regex pass in redactUrls: `/(https?:\/\/[^\s#'"<>]*)#[^\s'"<>]*=[^\s'"<>]*/g` → `$1#[redacted]`. Runs after the query-string pass so
  a URL with both a query and a fragment (`?q=1#t=abc`) gets both
  redacted (`?[redacted]#[redacted]`).
- URL portion in step 2 allows `?` so a previously-redacted query
  (`?[redacted]`) is included in the URL match and not broken.

3 new tests: OAuth fragment redacted, anchor + hash-route preserved,
query-and-fragment both redacted. 79 tests total (up from 76).

commits.subject: feat(debug): redact URL query strings in debug reports + env output
commits.body:
Users paste debug reports into support chats, and screenshot the pill
when the format is more convenient. Query strings routinely carry auth
tokens, session ids, and PII — both output paths need redaction before
leaving the device.

- New redactUrls(text) helper exported from debugLog.ts. Replaces
  everything from `?` up to the next whitespace / `#` / quote / angle
  bracket with `?[redacted]`. The fragment is preserved (client-side,
  usually non-sensitive).
- Regex intentionally excludes quotes and angle brackets from the
  query-match character class: without that, URLs embedded in
  JSON-stringified details (`{"url":"https://x/?t=1"}`) would have the
  regex greedily consume the closing `"}` as part of the "query" and
  produce malformed output.
- Applied in TWO places:
  - debugGetEnvironment: redacts env.url at construction time so the
    pill's Env tab can't leak the query in a screenshot. A caller that
    needs the raw URL reads globalThis.location.href directly.
  - debugGenerateReport: final sweep over the joined string so URLs
    that appear in log entry events / details get redacted too.

Tightening from the task snippet's regex (`[^\s#]*` for the query
chars): expanded to `[^\s#'"<>]*` to fix the JSON-embedded URL case
noted above. Fragment-preservation behaviour of the original is kept.

7 new tests — straight redaction / fragment preservation / no-query
passthrough / multi-URL / JSON-embedded / structured-detail via
report / env.url via report (location mocked).

commits.body:

- ~10 flatten real structure into a string (err instanceof Error ?
  err.message : String(err); JSON.stringify({...}); ErrorEvent.message
  dropping filename/lineno/colno).
- ~50 just pass a plain message string — no structure to preserve.

Wrapping the latter group as { message: str } would be mechanical
noise across ~20 unrelated files for no queryability gain. So instead
of migrating the literal "details: string → Record<string, unknown>"

migrated only the structure-losing callsites.

  of discarding everything but message.

commits.subject: refactor(debug): debugSubscribe replays entries + isolates subscriber errors
commits.body:
Architectural tightening of the pub/sub surface.

Problem: every subscriber had to do two things on mount — call
debugGetEntries() for the initial snapshot, then debugSubscribe() for
live updates. Easy to forget; left a race window where an entry added
between the two calls was visible to neither path. Separately, a
throwing subscriber would break subsequent subscribers in the same
notification loop (listeners.forEach with no try/catch).

Changes:
- Subscriber type: () => void → (entries: DebugEntry[]) => void.
- debugSubscribe now invokes the callback synchronously with the
  current snapshot before returning. Wrapped in try/catch.
- notifyAll() helper used by debugAdd/debugClear; iterates a
  snapshot of subscribers (so a self-unsubscribing callback doesn't
  disrupt iteration) and wraps each call in try/catch.
- Error isolation: a throwing subscriber never prevents others from
  receiving a notification, and never propagates out of debugAdd
  or debugSubscribe.
- DebugPill.useEffect collapses to a single debugSubscribe((latest)
  => { setEntries([...latest]); setCounts(debugGetCounts()); }).
  Removed the manual pre-subscribe sync and the now-unused
  debugGetEntries import.

Tests:
- Updated 'does not recurse when subscriber calls console.error' —
  immediate replay means the subscriber fires once during subscribe
  too, producing an additional 'subscriber log' entry. Adjusted
  expectations.
- New 'invokes the callback synchronously with the current snapshot
  (immediate replay)'.
- New 'isolates subscriber errors: one throwing subscriber does not
  block others'.

web: clean.

Deviation from task snippet: task noted "wrap each subscribers.forEach
in try/catch" — I extracted a single notifyAll helper used by both
debugAdd and debugClear so the isolation policy lives in one place.

commits.subject: feat(debug): pre-React inline boot pill for silent-boot-failure visibility
commits.body:
A broken JS bundle, parse error, or unhandled rejection during boot
previously produced a silent white screen. This adds a classic inline
IIFE in +html.tsx <head>, registered before the Metro bundle, that
shows a small red bottom-right banner in those cases.

Pill shows on:
- window.error (native script errors, React render crashes that bubble)
- unhandledrejection (async boot failures)
- 20s fallback timer — last-resort "App failed to load" indicator
  (preempted by the existing 15s recovery reload on first load; kicks
  in once the recovery counter caps at 2 reload attempts)

Exposes two hooks on window:
- __debugPushError(msg): lets app code surface errors to the pre-React
  pill if it wants to
- __debugClearLoadTimer(): called by RootLayoutNav's mount effect
  alongside setting __appHealthy. Cancels the 20s timer AND removes
  any pill that already appeared during boot — otherwise a transient
  early error that resolves on mount would leave a stale red banner.

Deviation from task snippet: the task only cleared the timer. I also
remove the DOM node on clear, because "app loaded but a banner says
it didn't" is strictly worse than showing nothing. Kept the 20s
timeout per the task despite knowing the 15s recovery preempts it
first-load — it's useful on the final reload attempt.

Structural verification: in the built dist/index.html, the boot IIFE
sits at byte 1570 (first inline script), before earlyInstallCapture
(1885), recoveryScript (2081), and the bundle <script src> (31906).

commits.subject: feat(debug): capture console.error / console.warn in the debug log
commits.body:
Patches console.error / console.warn at module load (web-only) so the
Log tab reflects everything React and app code log at error/warn
severity — PropType failures, hydration mismatches, key warnings,
caught exceptions, etc. — alongside manual debugAdd calls.

- Add 'console' to the DebugSource union; add a zinc-gray hex to the
  SOURCE palette in DebugPill.
- describeConsoleArgs() maps the first arg to the entry's `event`
  (truncated at 160 chars) and subsequent args to `details`.
- formatArg() serialises args safely: Error → stack/message, primitive
  → String(), object → JSON.stringify with a circular-ref replacer
  that also collapses React elements to '[ReactElement]' (would
  otherwise dump huge objects). Unserialisable values fall back to
  '[Unserializable]'.
- reentrant flag prevents infinite loops (e.g. a React subscriber
  logs a console.warn during render, which would retrigger the patch).
- __debugPatched sentinel makes the patch idempotent under Fast
  Refresh; without it, a module re-execute would wrap the already-
  wrapped console.error as the "original" and eventually recurse.
- Native skipped: RN's LogBox patches console itself at RN boot and
  module order is unreliable — whoever patches last wins, so cross-
  platform capture would silently lose entries mid-session.
- Original console.error / console.warn are still called afterwards,
  so DevTools' red/yellow message rendering is preserved.

Deviation from task snippet: task used debugAdd({ ... }) object form
and details: { args } (raw object). Existing debugAdd is positional
with details: string, used at 10 callsites. Reconciled by writing a
small helper that respects the existing API instead of migrating it.

commits.body:
Three related improvements, one commit:

1. Extract useArrowNavigation hook

   the same behavior, so extract into a shared hook alongside
   useDisclosureFocus and useFocusTrap. Reuses FOCUSABLE_SELECTOR for

2. Thread search button ref through SearchModalProvider
   The SearchButton lives in HeaderFilters; the search modal body

   useDisclosureFocus return focus to the button on close instead of
   dropping to document.body. useDisclosureFocus gained an optional
   overrides argument for this cross-component case.

   Panel gets role="dialog", aria-modal="true", id="search-panel",
   and accessibilityLabel="Search location". SearchButton gets

inside BurgerMenu, focus-return semantics need discussion.

commits.subject: feat(a11y): arrow keys + aria-controls on burger menu
commits.body:
Arrow/Home/End cycle between focusable menu items while the menu is
open. Home jumps to the first, End to the last; ArrowUp wraps past the
top, ArrowDown wraps past the bottom. Handler attaches to the menu
container (via containerRef) and short-circuits on native.

Selector-based query (same as useFocusTrap) instead of role="menuitem"
— this menu is a WAI-ARIA disclosure, not a role="menu" application
menu (see file header for rationale). role="menuitem" without a parent
role="menu" would be invalid ARIA and contradict the documented design.

aria-controls wires the trigger to the panel. Panel id is spread
web-only; on native, the Pressable doesn't receive unknown props.
aria-expanded continues to come from accessibilityState.expanded.
The panel is conditionally mounted — aria-controls resolves at
activation time per WAI-ARIA, so the stale reference is fine.

Theme toggle task was already implemented (lines 266-286) — no
changes needed there.

commits.subject: feat(a11y): keyboard focus for BurgerMenu disclosure
commits.body:
Adds two reusable hooks for the standard disclosure pattern:
- useDisclosureFocus: moves focus to the first item on open, returns it
  to the trigger on close.
- useFocusTrap: cycles Tab / Shift+Tab within the container while open.

Hooks follow the project convention of a plain .ts native stub plus a
.web.ts implementation — DOM-only APIs are isolated from native bundles
and the consumer sees a single import path on both platforms.

Wired into BurgerMenu: triggerRef on the hamburger Pressable, firstItemRef
on the "How to use" item, containerRef on the menu card. Other disclosures
(header dropdowns, filter panels) can adopt the same hooks later.

Public ref types are React.RefObject<any> — RN Web forwards refs to the
underlying DOM node on web and to the RN component instance on native,
so a permissive type is honest rather than requiring every call site
to cast.

  event consumption, granular 8-type browser detection, richer API

  branded offline fallback page

- _layout.tsx: early SW registration + backup prompt capture at module scope

  Chrome-on-iOS redirect to Safari, analytics tracking
- vercel.json: explicit SW/manifest rewrites, Service-Worker-Allowed,
  Content-Type headers, must-revalidate cache control

commits.subject: fix(pwa): fix post-deployment broken app state
commits.body:
After every Vercel deployment, the PWA got stuck showing no header, no map,
no data, and an offline banner. The only recovery was clearing app data.

Root causes:
- sw.js was a static file that never changed between deployments, so the
  browser never detected a new SW and never showed an update banner
- staleWhileRevalidate for HTML served cached old HTML referencing new JS
  bundle hashes not yet in cache
- SW returned empty Response('', {status: 503}) for failed JS fetches,
  causing silent partial rendering (components fail without visible errors)
- No self-recovery mechanism — with no header visible, users were stuck

Fixes:
- SW: Build-time version injection (inject-sw-version.mjs) so browser
  detects new SW after each deployment
- SW: Auto skipWaiting() on install — broken UI can't show "Update" banner
- SW: Network-first for HTML navigation — always fresh HTML when online
- SW: Versioned cache names — old caches cleaned on activation
- SW: Removed silent 503 fallback — let JS errors propagate naturally
- SW: Proper offline fallback page for navigation requests
- usePWAUpdate: controllerchange listener auto-reloads when new SW activates
- +html.tsx: Inline recovery script forces immediate SW update check +
  fallback cache clear after 15s if app fails to mount
- _layout.tsx: Sets __appHealthy flag for recovery script coordination

commits.subject: fix(pwa): remove unconditional skipWaiting from SW install handler
commits.body:
The install event called self.skipWaiting() unconditionally, which
auto-activated every new service worker immediately on install. This
bypassed the prompt-based update flow — the user never saw the "New
version available" banner because the SW activated before the UI could
detect the waiting state.

Now the new SW stays in waiting state until the user clicks "Update"
in PWABanner, which sends the SKIP_WAITING message. This matches the
registerType: 'prompt' pattern from the glow-props PWA spec.

commits.body:

variables that Uniwind updates on theme change:

- body background: var(--color-base-200, <hex fallback>)
- input focus ring: var(--color-primary, <hex fallback>)

during overscroll), and focus rings used the wrong color on non-default

commits.subject: feat(theme): auto-generate hex colors from DaisyUI oklch definitions
commits.body:
Add build script (scripts/generate-theme-colors.mjs) that reads oklch
values from daisyui/theme/object.js and converts to hex via culori
(CSS Color Level 4 gamut mapping). Replaces manual oklch→hex conversion
that had incorrect values for out-of-sRGB-gamut themes (abyss was
significantly wrong — #6FE744 should have been #BEFF00).

Also adds runtime meta theme-color update in useTheme.ts so browser
chrome color changes when user switches themes.

- Build script reads from daisyui npm package (devDep)
- culori (devDep) for accurate CSS Color Level 4 gamut mapping

commits.subject: fix(mobile-web): bottom sheet touch, burger menu position, PWA install visibility
commits.body:
Three mobile web fixes:

1. Bottom sheet inaccessible: Added touch-action:none to drag handle so
   mobile browsers don't intercept swipes as page scroll. Fixed content
   PanResponder that captured ALL vertical drags when sheet wasn't full —
   now only captures drag-down at scroll top (collapse) or drag-up when
   not full (expand), allowing FlatList scrolling at all sheet positions.

2. Burger menu on left: RN Web Modal doesn't reliably support position:fixed
   backdrop + position:absolute child on mobile browsers. Switched to flexbox
   alignment (alignItems:flex-end + marginRight) so menu anchors to the right
   edge regardless of Modal container width.

3. PWA install banner invisible: Was floating at bottom:190 over the map where
   it's easily missed or hidden behind the bottom sheet. Moved inside the
   bottom sheet content so it's visible when users interact with the station
   list. Increased auto-hide from 10s to 30s for slower mobile interactions.

commits.subject: fix(data): add HTTPS proxy support to geocode-all script
commits.body:
Node.js https.get does not respect the https_proxy/HTTPS_PROXY env var
(unlike curl). In proxy-only environments (CI containers, sandboxed
hosts), direct TCP connections fail with DNS errors while curl succeeds
through the proxy tunnel.

Added HTTP CONNECT tunneling: when https_proxy is set, the script
establishes a CONNECT tunnel through the proxy, then makes HTTPS
requests through the tunneled socket. Falls back to direct connections
when no proxy is configured.

commits.subject: feat(data): geocode all stations — no import without country validation
commits.body:
Previously the pipeline split stations into two paths: "accepted" (had
address+city from OSM tags, auto-labeled ZA) and "skipped" (needed
geocoding). This meant ~95% of border-zone stations without addr:country
tags could enter the import as ZA without any validation.

New approach: every station gets geocoded via Nominatim.
- Transform (step 2) now outputs all elements for geocoding, only
  rejecting those explicitly tagged as non-SA
- geocode-all.mjs (step 3) validates country_code for every station
  and merges data: OSM for street-level detail, Nominatim for admin
  hierarchy (city, suburb, province, country)
- Province coverage jumps from ~40% to ~100%
- Expected runtime: ~70 min for ~4,200 stations (resumable via cache)

Merge strategy tested with mock data — OSM street address wins,
Nominatim admin hierarchy wins, OSM postal code wins with Nominatim
fallback.

commits.subject: fix(data): add 3-layer country filter with border zone detection
commits.body:
The addr:country tag only covers ~5% of stations near borders (tested
on 110 elements in Eswatini border zone). Untagged stations with valid
address/city were being auto-accepted as ZA — a confirmed data integrity
bug.

Three-layer filter:
1. addr:country tag rejects non-ZA when present
2. Border zone detection routes untagged near-border stations to reverse
   geocoding instead of auto-accepting
3. Nominatim reverse geocode validates country_code === 'za'

Tested: Eswatini border zone — 5 accepted (tagged ZA), 104 routed to
geocode (untagged), 0 SA stations wrongly excluded.

commits.subject: fix(data): replace area filter with bbox + post-filter for reliability
commits.body:
The Overpass area filter area["ISO3166-1"="ZA"] was untested and carries
risks: slower polygon queries, higher timeout risk with way/relation
elements, and temporary unavailability if the ZA boundary relation is
being edited.

Switched to the proven bbox approach with a two-layer country filter:
1. Transform rejects stations with addr:country != ZA
2. Reverse geocode validates country_code === 'za' from Nominatim

This is faster, more reliable, and can be switched to area filter later
if bbox leakage proves significant.

commits.body:
Identified 9 gaps in the current pipeline after reviewing the transform
script and RPC function:

1. Missing way/relation OSM elements (only fetching nodes)
2. No dedup — RPC does plain INSERT, re-runs create duplicates

8. Reverse geocode script not yet implemented
9. No validation of geocode results

commits.subject: feat(data): add Overpass API transform script for OSM fuel station import
commits.body:
Adds a Node.js script that transforms raw Overpass API data (OpenStreetMap
fuel stations in South Africa) into the bulk-import JSON format accepted by
the bulk_insert_stations RPC function.

The Overpass API provides 4,215 fuel stations for SA in a single query.
The transform maps OSM tags to our station schema: brand (85%), phone (63%),
amenities, operating hours, loyalty programs, payment methods, and province.

2,733 stations pass validation with 0 errors. The remaining 1,482 lack
address/city data in OSM and would need reverse geocoding to fill in.

Output is written as batched JSON files (1000 per batch) for safe import
via the admin bulk-import UI.

Usage:
  curl -sL --max-time 120 -X POST \
    -d 'data=[out:json][timeout:100];node[amenity=fuel](-35,16,-22,33);out body;' \
    "https://overpass-api.de/api/interpreter" -o data/overpass-raw.json
  node scripts/transform-overpass.mjs

commits.body:
PWA system from CLAUDE.md Suggested Implementations, adapted for Expo Web:
- Service worker (public/sw.js): cache-first for static assets, network-first
  for Supabase API, stale-while-revalidate for navigation. Version-based cache.

  hasUpdate boolean and updateApp() to activate waiting SW.
- usePWAInstall hook: detects browser type (Chromium/Safari/Firefox), captures
  beforeinstallprompt event, persists dismiss state. Includes race condition
  fix via early-capture inline script in +html.tsx.
- InstallPrompt banner: bottom banner with "Install" (Chromium native) or
  "How to Install" (Safari/Firefox manual instructions).
- InstallInstructionsModal: browser-specific step-by-step guides (Safari iOS,
  Safari macOS, Firefox Android, Firefox desktop).
- UpdatePrompt banner: teal bar at top when new SW version is waiting.
- manifest.json background_color updated to dark theme (#111827).

Timer leak fixes (from CLAUDE.md Suggested Implementations):
- BurgerMenu: track handleItem setTimeout in ref, cleanup on unmount
- DebugPill: cleanup auto-scroll timeout in useEffect return

commits.body:
Alpha-phase diagnostic tool for inspecting app behavior on shared/mobile
devices where console access is unavailable.

  unhandled errors/rejections at module load.

- useStations: station search success/failure
- useLocation: GPS permission, acquisition, failure
- useFuelTypes: fuel types fetch success/failure
- FuelMapWeb: map init success/failure

commits.subject: feat(init): scaffold Expo project with Supabase schema and geospatial queries
commits.body:
Project initialization for the fuel station finder app:
- Expo SDK 54 with TypeScript, file-based routing (expo-router), tabs template
- Supabase schema: fuel_types, stations (PostGIS), fuel_prices, price_history
- Materialized view (current_prices) with auto-refresh trigger
- RLS policies: public read for active data, admin full access via JWT role
- find_stations_in_radius() PostgreSQL function for geospatial radius search
- Seed data: 6 SA fuel types, 12 Gauteng stations with sample prices
- Supabase client (src/lib/supabase.ts) and app-level TypeScript types
- Route stubs for station detail and admin views
- .env.example with required env var names

Docker daemon unavailable in this environment so migrations are untested
against a live database — SQL follows the spec exactly and is ready to run
via `supabase start` or against a remote Supabase project.
